feed.title: Eng (Sambo) Boris
feed.subtitle: Eng Boris's webpage. PhD in Computer Science.

entry.title: On Intuitionism and Classicism
entry.content:
Prerequisite: natural deduction, sequent calculus, intuitionistic logic, classical logic

Introduction

Is there several logics? A unique and right logic? Intuitionism was introduced during the so-called foundational crisis of mathematics in the beginning of the 20th century by mathematicians such as H. Poincaré. Strongly defended by L.E.J Brouwer [1], it constitutes both a critic of the mathematical practice and an opposition to formalism in favor of intuition and construction. I provide an understanding of intuitionism and classicism to the ones not familiar with it but who already know it. We start from the question of the reliability of logic and mathematics.

The location of certainty

Practice shows that mathematics has a great reliability but in the time of Brouwer, the existence of non-euclidean geometries resulted in the emergence of doubts regarding this reliability. What right allows us to put a proposition as axiom or to use a particular rule of deduction? Where does the certainty of mathematics lies? Two school of philosophy tried to answer that last question:

  • Formalism (German): it is located in the paper, that is in the symbols we write, in the linguistics of mathematics, which is concrete, elementary and combinatorial
  • Intuitionism (French): it is located in the human’s intuition, that is, we should rely to what is accessible to our intuition, what we can know as humans

Something still links both of them: the rejection of the correlation between mathematical laws and the laws of nature. In other words they’re both strongly subjectivist conceptions of logic and mathematics. We focus on intuitionism and its viewpoint regarding logic.

Subjectivism and constructivism

Science rely on empirical entities thus defining certainty on observation and experience. It may be a good start to find where is the certainty of logic. From the viewpoint of intuitionism, certainty appears when we can make the existence of mathematical entities explicit (opposing semantic realism and its external existence of entities). It has several consequences:

  • A review of infinity. Brouwer rejected actual infinity (e.g infinite enumeration) but admitted potential infinity (e.g the inductive definitions).
  • The rejection of the law of excluded-middle $\forall P. P \lor \lnot P$ because it automatically provide either a proof of $A$ or $\lnot A$ both coming right from nowhere.
  • The rejection of proof by contradiction (i.e $\lnot A \Rightarrow \bot \vdash A$) and several other laws for the same reason.
  • A proof of existence $\exists x. P(x)$ must provide explicitly an entity $c$ and a proof of $P(c)$

The rejection of logical laws was quite shocking since they were both strongly used in the mathematical practice. Intuitionism is a subjectivist ideology because it only accept what we, humans, can know, understand and construct, opposing any theory of truth and realism (adherence to a reality). We have to accept that we don’t necessarily know and have to consider the undecidability of propositions (a fatality).

Brouwer defended pure intuition instead of a linguistical and mechanical thus belittling view of mathematics so he was against any formalist conception of logic. Ironically, himself and one of his students, A. Heyting (and independently A. Kolmogorov) are behind the formalization of constructivism through the BHK interpretation. Logic is now divided in two: classical logic and intuitionistic logic.

Now, let’s explore the technical content of intuitionism.

Technical interpretation

Model-theoretic interpretation

Classical logic is naturally interpreted with truth values either $0$ or $1$ associated to a logical proposition. As the concept of provability changed in the intuitionistic framework, we have to switch to Heyting models or Kripke models internalizing the unknowable and ignoring the deep mechanism (dynamics) of intuitionism.

In Heyting models we usually interpret propositions in either a symbol representing truth, falsity or an “intermediate truth values” (e.g ordered by inclusion). These values represent the absence of knowledge “I don’t know” that we can assign to generally undecidable propositions such as the law of excluded middle. In Kripke models we do something similar by reifying a “world of all possibilities” allowing us to model the unknown/unknowable.

Proof-theoretic interpretation

The proof-theoretical interpretation is a bit more interesting. Let’s explore it in the Natural Deduction $NJ$ and the Sequent Calculus $LJ$.

  • The system $NJ$ is inherently and naturally intuitionistic because it simulates reasoning with implicit structural rules on the context and with determinism in the unique conclusion. Any reference to sub-formula in the final statement already appears proved in the top of the derivation tree i.e everything is explicitly justified at some point. We can make it classical by adding a classical law but it’s indeed an artificial extension which also breaks the symmetry introduction/elimination since the excluded middle is a kind of introduction of disjunction. We should not break it since it is responsible of the good behavior of proof-reduction.

  • The sequent calculus is especially interesting because we can make it either classical or intuitionistic by restricting the right-hand part of the sequent to at most one formula or an arbitrary number of formulas. The system $LK$ has sequents of the shape $\Gamma \vdash \Delta$ for arbitrary sequences of formulas $\Gamma, \Delta$ and $LJ$ of the shape $\Gamma \vdash A$. In other words, we forbid contraction (infinity/duplication) in the conclusion. This peculiarity is not innocent and purely technical, the right-space restriction allows to focus only on a unique conclusion while laws such as the excluded-middle allows to diverge by kind of “jumps” in the reasoning. Having the focus only on the construction of the conclusion enforces constructivity.

Computational interpretation

The system $NJ$ being isomorphic to the $\lambda$-calculus through the Curry-Howard isomorphism, the computational interpretation is straightforward:

  • Intuitionistic proofs $\Gamma \vdash A$ are typed functional programs $\Gamma \vdash M : A$.
  • Lambda terms can also be seen as a kind of logs or annotation describing the structure of the proof.

In particular, intuitionism captures computational decidability. What is provable is what is computable by a sequential functional program. We have a correspondence between computationally decidable and logically decidable in this framework. As in $NJ$, the computation only operates sequentially on a unique output.

Classicism strikes back

Is intuitionism weaker due to its rejection of logical rules?

No, intuitionism isn’t weaker. It can even be seen as stronger because intuitionistic logic is a logic where only constructions emerges. Not only it has a power that classical logic don’t have but moreover, the conception of classical truth isn’t lost (we can encode classical provability in the intuitionistic framework).

To recover the classical truth, there exists several encoding called double-negation translations such that if $A$ is provable classically then is provable in intuitionistic logic. These translations usually work by putting double-negations $\lnot\lnot$ at the right position in formulas. Here is an example with a proof of the encoding of the excluded-middle in natural deduction $NJ$ and sequent calculus $LJ$.

Note that we have $\lnot A \equiv A \Rightarrow \bot$ in intuitionistic systems. We also use the notation $\Gamma = {(A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot}$.

$ \infer[\Rightarrow i]{ \infer[\Rightarrow e]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad}{\Gamma \vdash (A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot} \qquad \infer[\lor i]{ \infer[\Rightarrow i]{ \infer[\Rightarrow e]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad}{\Gamma, A \vdash (A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot} \qquad \infer[\lor i]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad\qquad\qquad}{\Gamma, A \vdash A} }{\Gamma, A \vdash A \lor \lnot A} }{\Gamma, A \vdash \bot} }{\Gamma \vdash \lnot A} }{\Gamma \vdash A \lor \lnot A} } {(A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot \vdash \bot} }{\vdash_{NJ} ((A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot) \Rightarrow \bot} $


$ \infer[\Rightarrow r]{ \infer[c]{ \infer[\Rightarrow l]{ \infer[\lor r]{ \infer[\Rightarrow r]{ \infer[\Rightarrow l]{ \infer[\lor l]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad}{A \vdash A} }{A \vdash A \lor \lnot A} \qquad \infer[ax]{\qquad}{\bot \vdash \bot} }{\Gamma, A \vdash \bot} }{\Gamma \vdash \lnot A} }{\Gamma \vdash A \lor \lnot A} \qquad \infer[ax]{\qquad}{\bot \vdash \bot} }{(A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot, (A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot \vdash \bot} }{(A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot \vdash \bot} }{\vdash_{LJ} ((A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot) \Rightarrow \bot} $

These translations not only recover the classical power of proofs but also gives a computational interpretation of proofs. Why does $\vdash \lnot\lnot(A \lor \lnot A)$ works? Double-negation isn’t a magical technicality coming from nowhere. Actually, it works because adding negations allows to bypass the restriction on conclusion and exploit free contraction on the hypothesis. We clearly see that negation act as a spatial manipulation in a proof (it puts information in the context). Double-negation make the context-awareness of proofs and programs emerge (as we can see in CPS translations).

If we take a close look at the two above proofs, we can see that a kind of mechanism of “saving of information” is at work. The double-negation allows us to put information in the context which is less restricted and thus we can reuse it for both the branches with $A$ and the one with $\lnot A$ in $A \lor \lnot A$ where in the intuitionistic case we had a choice destructing information (irreversibility of disjunction).

Going further, we can see if we prove classical laws by each other that double-negation corresponds to an artificial ability of freely saving information or an application of pointers/jumps on data while maintaining constructivity and sequentiality. The Curry-Howard isomorphism tells us that the computational contents of classical logic lies in continuation-passing style and control operators such as call/cc.

Conclusion: classical logic and non-determinism

To conclude, right-hand restriction on space forbids infinity on the conclusion but enforces constructivity and sequentiality. We may think that some classical truths are lost in this new intuitionistic framework but an encoding with double-negation allows to simulate classical proofs in a sequential way by a mechanism of jump/pointer/saving on the left-hand side which acts as a kind of memory.

But still, classical logic remains the most natural way of reasoning for humans, why? Intuitionism fits the point of view of sequential machines but pure classicism apparently don’t. What makes $\vdash A \lor \lnot A$ provable? It seems to be the ability of retraction (which is destructive for sequential machines). The excluded-middle is indeed provable if we choose to prove $\lnot A$, then introduce $A$ in the context and change our mind to prove $A$ instead (as suggested by P. Wadler’s devil):

$ \infer[\lor r]{ \infer[\lnot r]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad}{A \vdash A} }{\vdash A, \lnot A} }{\vdash_{LK} A \lor \lnot A} $

But $LK$ lacking confluence as stated by the below critical pair [2], it is irrelevant in the computational world.

$ \infer[cut]{ \infer[RW]{ \infer{\vdots}{A \vdash B} }{A \vdash C, B} \qquad \infer[LW]{ \infer{\vdots}{D \vdash E} }{D, C \vdash E} }{A, D \vdash B, E} $

reducing to either

$\infer[{RW, RC, LW, LC}^\ast]{A, D \vdash B, E}{A \vdash B}$

or

$\infer[{RW, RC, LW, LC}^\ast]{A, D \vdash B, E}{D \vdash E}$

To study logical phenomena further we can normalize the rules of $LK$ thanks to the involutivity of negation to only consider right rules producing a monolateral version of $LK$. Sequents are now of the shape $\vdash A_1, …, A_n$ corresponding to a kind of space of concurrent interaction with partial/unfinished proofs we can postpone:

  • the cut ${\small\infer[cut]{\vdash \Gamma, A \quad \vdash \Delta, \lnot A}{\vdash \Gamma, \Delta}}$ makes $A$ interacts with its dual $\lnot A$.
  • general infinity (free duplication/contraction and erasure/weakening) creates non-determinism leading to complex exponentiation and polarization in Linear Logic.
  • the goal is to connect two dual formulas by the axiom $\vdash \Gamma, A, \lnot A$.

Classicism gives more space to work on proofs and seems to be environment-aware for both pure classicism and negation-translations. Finally, one may ask what is the status of concurrency and parallelism in reasoning and computation and if consciousness is sequential and unconsciousness parallel.

References

[1] Brouwer, L. E. J. (1913). Intuitionism and Formalism. Bulletin of the American Mathematical Society 20:81-96.

[2] Girard, J. Y., Taylor, P., & Lafont, Y. (1989). Proofs and types (Vol. 7). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
entry.summary: Prerequisite: natural deduction, sequent calculus, intuitionistic logic, classical logic Introduction Is there several logics? A unique and right logic? Intuitionism was introduced during the so-called foundational crisis of mathematics in the beginning of the 20th century by mathematicians such as H. Poincaré. Strongly defended by L.E.J Brouwer [1], it constitutes both a critic of the mathematical practice and an opposition to formalism in favor of intuition and construction. I provide an understanding of intuitionism and classicism to the ones not familiar with it but who already know it. We start from the question of the reliability of logic and mathematics. The location of certainty Practice shows that mathematics has a great reliability but in the time of Brouwer, the existence of non-euclidean geometries resulted in the emergence of doubts regarding this reliability. What right allows us to put a proposition as axiom or to use a particular rule of deduction? Where does the certainty of mathematics lies? Two school of philosophy tried to answer that last question: Formalism (German): it is located in the paper, that is in the symbols we write, in the linguistics of mathematics, which is concrete, elementary and combinatorial Intuitionism (French): it is located in the human’s intuition, that is, we should rely to what is accessible to our intuition, what we can know as humans Something still links both of them: the rejection of the correlation between mathematical laws and the laws of nature. In other words they’re both strongly subjectivist conceptions of logic and mathematics. We focus on intuitionism and its viewpoint regarding logic. Subjectivism and constructivism Science rely on empirical entities thus defining certainty on observation and experience. It may be a good start to find where is the certainty of logic. From the viewpoint of intuitionism, certainty appears when we can make the existence of mathematical entities explicit (opposing semantic realism and its external existence of entities). It has several consequences: A review of infinity. Brouwer rejected actual infinity (e.g infinite enumeration) but admitted potential infinity (e.g the inductive definitions). The rejection of the law of excluded-middle $\forall P. P \lor \lnot P$ because it automatically provide either a proof of $A$ or $\lnot A$ both coming right from nowhere. The rejection of proof by contradiction (i.e $\lnot A \Rightarrow \bot \vdash A$) and several other laws for the same reason. A proof of existence $\exists x. P(x)$ must provide explicitly an entity $c$ and a proof of $P(c)$ The rejection of logical laws was quite shocking since they were both strongly used in the mathematical practice. Intuitionism is a subjectivist ideology because it only accept what we, humans, can know, understand and construct, opposing any theory of truth and realism (adherence to a reality). We have to accept that we don’t necessarily know and have to consider the undecidability of propositions (a fatality). Brouwer defended pure intuition instead of a linguistical and mechanical thus belittling view of mathematics so he was against any formalist conception of logic. Ironically, himself and one of his students, A. Heyting (and independently A. Kolmogorov) are behind the formalization of constructivism through the BHK interpretation. Logic is now divided in two: classical logic and intuitionistic logic. Now, let’s explore the technical content of intuitionism. Technical interpretation Model-theoretic interpretation Classical logic is naturally interpreted with truth values either $0$ or $1$ associated to a logical proposition. As the concept of provability changed in the intuitionistic framework, we have to switch to Heyting models or Kripke models internalizing the unknowable and ignoring the deep mechanism (dynamics) of intuitionism. In Heyting models we usually interpret propositions in either a symbol representing truth, falsity or an “intermediate truth values” (e.g ordered by inclusion). These values represent the absence of knowledge “I don’t know” that we can assign to generally undecidable propositions such as the law of excluded middle. In Kripke models we do something similar by reifying a “world of all possibilities” allowing us to model the unknown/unknowable. Proof-theoretic interpretation The proof-theoretical interpretation is a bit more interesting. Let’s explore it in the Natural Deduction $NJ$ and the Sequent Calculus $LJ$. The system $NJ$ is inherently and naturally intuitionistic because it simulates reasoning with implicit structural rules on the context and with determinism in the unique conclusion. Any reference to sub-formula in the final statement already appears proved in the top of the derivation tree i.e everything is explicitly justified at some point. We can make it classical by adding a classical law but it’s indeed an artificial extension which also breaks the symmetry introduction/elimination since the excluded middle is a kind of introduction of disjunction. We should not break it since it is responsible of the good behavior of proof-reduction. The sequent calculus is especially interesting because we can make it either classical or intuitionistic by restricting the right-hand part of the sequent to at most one formula or an arbitrary number of formulas. The system $LK$ has sequents of the shape $\Gamma \vdash \Delta$ for arbitrary sequences of formulas $\Gamma, \Delta$ and $LJ$ of the shape $\Gamma \vdash A$. In other words, we forbid contraction (infinity/duplication) in the conclusion. This peculiarity is not innocent and purely technical, the right-space restriction allows to focus only on a unique conclusion while laws such as the excluded-middle allows to diverge by kind of “jumps” in the reasoning. Having the focus only on the construction of the conclusion enforces constructivity. Computational interpretation The system $NJ$ being isomorphic to the $\lambda$-calculus through the Curry-Howard isomorphism, the computational interpretation is straightforward: Intuitionistic proofs $\Gamma \vdash A$ are typed functional programs $\Gamma \vdash M : A$. Lambda terms can also be seen as a kind of logs or annotation describing the structure of the proof. In particular, intuitionism captures computational decidability. What is provable is what is computable by a sequential functional program. We have a correspondence between computationally decidable and logically decidable in this framework. As in $NJ$, the computation only operates sequentially on a unique output. Classicism strikes back Is intuitionism weaker due to its rejection of logical rules? No, intuitionism isn’t weaker. It can even be seen as stronger because intuitionistic logic is a logic where only constructions emerges. Not only it has a power that classical logic don’t have but moreover, the conception of classical truth isn’t lost (we can encode classical provability in the intuitionistic framework). To recover the classical truth, there exists several encoding called double-negation translations such that if $A$ is provable classically then is provable in intuitionistic logic. These translations usually work by putting double-negations $\lnot\lnot$ at the right position in formulas. Here is an example with a proof of the encoding of the excluded-middle in natural deduction $NJ$ and sequent calculus $LJ$. Note that we have $\lnot A \equiv A \Rightarrow \bot$ in intuitionistic systems. We also use the notation $\Gamma = {(A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot}$. $ \infer[\Rightarrow i]{ \infer[\Rightarrow e]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad}{\Gamma \vdash (A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot} \qquad \infer[\lor i]{ \infer[\Rightarrow i]{ \infer[\Rightarrow e]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad}{\Gamma, A \vdash (A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot} \qquad \infer[\lor i]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad\qquad\qquad}{\Gamma, A \vdash A} }{\Gamma, A \vdash A \lor \lnot A} }{\Gamma, A \vdash \bot} }{\Gamma \vdash \lnot A} }{\Gamma \vdash A \lor \lnot A} } {(A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot \vdash \bot} }{\vdash_{NJ} ((A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot) \Rightarrow \bot} $ $ \infer[\Rightarrow r]{ \infer[c]{ \infer[\Rightarrow l]{ \infer[\lor r]{ \infer[\Rightarrow r]{ \infer[\Rightarrow l]{ \infer[\lor l]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad}{A \vdash A} }{A \vdash A \lor \lnot A} \qquad \infer[ax]{\qquad}{\bot \vdash \bot} }{\Gamma, A \vdash \bot} }{\Gamma \vdash \lnot A} }{\Gamma \vdash A \lor \lnot A} \qquad \infer[ax]{\qquad}{\bot \vdash \bot} }{(A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot, (A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot \vdash \bot} }{(A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot \vdash \bot} }{\vdash_{LJ} ((A \lor \lnot A) \Rightarrow \bot) \Rightarrow \bot} $ These translations not only recover the classical power of proofs but also gives a computational interpretation of proofs. Why does $\vdash \lnot\lnot(A \lor \lnot A)$ works? Double-negation isn’t a magical technicality coming from nowhere. Actually, it works because adding negations allows to bypass the restriction on conclusion and exploit free contraction on the hypothesis. We clearly see that negation act as a spatial manipulation in a proof (it puts information in the context). Double-negation make the context-awareness of proofs and programs emerge (as we can see in CPS translations). If we take a close look at the two above proofs, we can see that a kind of mechanism of “saving of information” is at work. The double-negation allows us to put information in the context which is less restricted and thus we can reuse it for both the branches with $A$ and the one with $\lnot A$ in $A \lor \lnot A$ where in the intuitionistic case we had a choice destructing information (irreversibility of disjunction). Going further, we can see if we prove classical laws by each other that double-negation corresponds to an artificial ability of freely saving information or an application of pointers/jumps on data while maintaining constructivity and sequentiality. The Curry-Howard isomorphism tells us that the computational contents of classical logic lies in continuation-passing style and control operators such as call/cc. Conclusion: classical logic and non-determinism To conclude, right-hand restriction on space forbids infinity on the conclusion but enforces constructivity and sequentiality. We may think that some classical truths are lost in this new intuitionistic framework but an encoding with double-negation allows to simulate classical proofs in a sequential way by a mechanism of jump/pointer/saving on the left-hand side which acts as a kind of memory. But still, classical logic remains the most natural way of reasoning for humans, why? Intuitionism fits the point of view of sequential machines but pure classicism apparently don’t. What makes $\vdash A \lor \lnot A$ provable? It seems to be the ability of retraction (which is destructive for sequential machines). The excluded-middle is indeed provable if we choose to prove $\lnot A$, then introduce $A$ in the context and change our mind to prove $A$ instead (as suggested by P. Wadler’s devil): $ \infer[\lor r]{ \infer[\lnot r]{ \infer[ax]{\qquad}{A \vdash A} }{\vdash A, \lnot A} }{\vdash_{LK} A \lor \lnot A} $ But $LK$ lacking confluence as stated by the below critical pair [2], it is irrelevant in the computational world. $ \infer[cut]{ \infer[RW]{ \infer{\vdots}{A \vdash B} }{A \vdash C, B} \qquad \infer[LW]{ \infer{\vdots}{D \vdash E} }{D, C \vdash E} }{A, D \vdash B, E} $ reducing to either $\infer[{RW, RC, LW, LC}^\ast]{A, D \vdash B, E}{A \vdash B}$ or $\infer[{RW, RC, LW, LC}^\ast]{A, D \vdash B, E}{D \vdash E}$ To study logical phenomena further we can normalize the rules of $LK$ thanks to the involutivity of negation to only consider right rules producing a monolateral version of $LK$. Sequents are now of the shape $\vdash A_1, …, A_n$ corresponding to a kind of space of concurrent interaction with partial/unfinished proofs we can postpone: the cut ${\small\infer[cut]{\vdash \Gamma, A \quad \vdash \Delta, \lnot A}{\vdash \Gamma, \Delta}}$ makes $A$ interacts with its dual $\lnot A$. general infinity (free duplication/contraction and erasure/weakening) creates non-determinism leading to complex exponentiation and polarization in Linear Logic. the goal is to connect two dual formulas by the axiom $\vdash \Gamma, A, \lnot A$. Classicism gives more space to work on proofs and seems to be environment-aware for both pure classicism and negation-translations. Finally, one may ask what is the status of concurrency and parallelism in reasoning and computation and if consciousness is sequential and unconsciousness parallel. References [1] Brouwer, L. E. J. (1913). Intuitionism and Formalism. Bulletin of the American Mathematical Society 20:81-96. [2] Girard, J. Y., Taylor, P., & Lafont, Y. (1989). Proofs and types (Vol. 7). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

entry.title: Questions (currently 12 available)
entry.content:
Here are some questions I’m currently interested in. These questions may be superficial, meaningless or may even have been already solved. This is what I typically write at night while being in a strange mental state so please don’t take it too seriously.

  1. How does logic and computation manipulate and is affected by space and time? Linear Logic seems to take logic from mental/artificial references to the primitive intuition of space and time. Mathematical proofs which were seen as sequential mental acts evolving through time became proof-nets describing topological manipulations operated by logic (thus not only a mere convenience). The lost of time is recovered in the works on polarization and focalization, exhibiting a mechanism of interaction by a dialogue between positives and negatives. How does interaction and information evolve in space and time? What can Logic teach us about the study of time (sequentiality and concurrency)? What can the study of space (graph theory, topology) teach us about the mechanisms of logic?

  2. Are reductionist approaches to logic limited? Should we prefer a holistic one? Is exploring the (compositional?) structure of Logic and Computation enough to understand them? Category Theory capture quite well logical and computational phenomenon but one can wonder if something does escape the categorical understanding. What are the limits of the categorical point of view? It seems that Category Theory makes some assumptions reflecting how we understand abstract concepts. Categorical entities are high-level and considered up-to-isomorphism thus forgetting location and altering identification (which is a concrete concept). Does the categorical view somehow format (positively? negatively?) our understanding?

  3. Does the pragmatic approach of computation solve the identity crisis of Logic? It was once thought that classical logic didn’t have any computational interpretation. Then came a quite surprising interpretation given by the logical interpretation of innocent instructions (such as call/cc from Scheme). Can everyday programming provide a significant creativity to Logic? What is the logical interpretation of usual entities of practical programming? Can the practical use of computer science and programming (which is pragmatic) change our understanding of Logic (always influenced by paths of directed, engaged ideas and our understanding of concepts)? It may be interesting to investigate the computational meaning of some mathematical axioms/theorems.

  4. How are Logic and Computation related to Cognition and Nature? Linear Logic is used in type systems for quantum functional programming languages but also to describe higher-order quantum circuits through proof nets. How is it connected to quantum computation and quantum information led by the laws of quantum mechanics? Is there really any connection at all? Is it an exaggeration to relate cognition, computation, nature, quantum, and logic? Logic and computation seem to be part of the nature but the nature itself doesn’t seem to truly compute or reason in a discrete framework (or maybe even not at all). More straightforward, are logic and computation part of the nature (i.e does the nature use it as well) or are they a purely artificial exploitation of its potential (e.g logic used as a purely subjective theory of normativity, computation as artificial automation).

  5. What is the origin of historical prejudices and misconceptions of Logic? What does the history of Logic teach us about the way humans think and what/why do they believe? Like Physics and Biology, should Logic be taken as a science (observing interaction as a natural phenomenon)? It may be interesting to investigate the biological, psychological and sociological aspects of Logic. Why was Logic thought and organized the way it was? How can the understanding of our failure prevent us from going in wrong directions?

  6. What makes something computationally difficult or easy? Implicit Computational Complexity allows to express computational complexity classes by syntactical restrictions on formal languages. It is then interesting to explore the deep structure of these restrictions. It seems that algebraic tools may give an understanding we didn’t have with the algorithmic view.

  7. What Logic and Computation really are and how are they similar or different? Even today it is not clear what computation and logic are even though we thought we understood several times. The Curry-Howard isomorphism tells us that logic and (converging coherent?) computation share a common structure and are deeply related. Something similar to what separate analytic and synthetic seem to deeply separate computation and what we call logic. Logic has both a restrictive (type systems à la Curry) and normative (type systems à la Church) effect on computational behaviors (untyped computation) which can be both formulated within Proof Theory thanks to the cut-elimination procedure. What about the purely locative and interactional Turing Machines? Where do they stand?

  8. What is the common ground of syntactical formats? Some formalisms took a particular format due to (mainly) historical reasons but are equivalent to other ones. For instance, inductive description of regular languages, finite state automata, regular grammars, regular expressions and read-only Turing machines all define regular languages. How can they be abstracted to only keep interactional potential and use of information/data? Although similar, these formats are far from being strictly/really equivalent. How do they differ? How can we produce a more fertile and simple syntax to describe a concept? How do concepts (specification) become syntax (usable/sharable entities)? Is a study of the administrative aspects of mathematical practice and formats possible? The BHK interpretation is such a concept involving several syntaxes.

  9. What is non-determinism and what is its role in the human/machine duality? A kind of non-determinism seems to exists within classical logic especially in the classical Sequent Calculus. Although there are a kind of connection between the two branches of the law of the excluded-middle (prove ~A then focus on A by retraction), to prove a statement of the shape (A or ~A) amount to try to prove A, then ~A, then return to A… (e.g P?=NP). We can’t predict the outcome (if there’s is one). We can also remark that classical logic (in its pure form) lack of computational content (non-confluency) but is the more natural way of reasoning for humans (even though it was rejected by intuitionism). Does all of that have any connection at all with the separation between humans and machines?

  10. How does mathematical concepts emerge from thoughts? How does intuitive concepts such as sets, numbers and functions emerges? Numbers for instance, have several definitions (Church, Parigot, Peano, Von Neumann or even composition of morphisms in monoids as unitary categories…). Is there any dialogue within our own mind (subject/subject) or with our environment (subject/object) ensuring this emergence? Is there any (well justified) classes of possible conceptions derived by interactions (either subject/subject or subject/object)? For instance, plurality, unity, existence, universality… What is the correlation between that and our conception of space and time? (are numbers emerging from our conception of time and sets from our conception of space?)

  11. What is the point of convergence of the dialectical evolution between humans and machines? The point zero is the point where humans first thought about automatizing their activities (before computers). Let’s call these automatizing mechanisms “machines”. Humans made machines to improve their life and freeing them from repetitive behaviors. The human life which could be defined over a single activity thus evolved by changing its activities. The more machines are improved, the more the concept of human intelligence is redefined. During the era of Artificial Intelligence, machines could approximate human intelligence but then redefined the meaning of intelligence itself in the process. The creation of challenges for machines implicitly lead to the improvement of humanity (always a step ahead of machines). Will the role of human and machines merge more and more through the time? Where does that dialectical evolution lead? Does it create conflicts with social and psychological aspects of humanity?

  12. What is the ontological status of the Internet? What is the meaning of the Internet? What does it compute? Thanks to the massive storage of data, the Internet seems to include a record of humanity constantly reflecting its current state. More than that, the Internet seems to globally approximate humanity and its knowledge. Can we see it as an embodiment of a brain of the human kind?
entry.summary: Here are some questions I’m currently interested in. These questions may be superficial, meaningless or may even have been already solved. This is what I typically write at night while being in a strange mental state so please don’t take it too seriously. How does logic and computation manipulate and is affected by space and time? Linear Logic seems to take logic from mental/artificial references to the primitive intuition of space and time. Mathematical proofs which were seen as sequential mental acts evolving through time became proof-nets describing topological manipulations operated by logic (thus not only a mere convenience). The lost of time is recovered in the works on polarization and focalization, exhibiting a mechanism of interaction by a dialogue between positives and negatives. How does interaction and information evolve in space and time? What can Logic teach us about the study of time (sequentiality and concurrency)? What can the study of space (graph theory, topology) teach us about the mechanisms of logic? Are reductionist approaches to logic limited? Should we prefer a holistic one? Is exploring the (compositional?) structure of Logic and Computation enough to understand them? Category Theory capture quite well logical and computational phenomenon but one can wonder if something does escape the categorical understanding. What are the limits of the categorical point of view? It seems that Category Theory makes some assumptions reflecting how we understand abstract concepts. Categorical entities are high-level and considered up-to-isomorphism thus forgetting location and altering identification (which is a concrete concept). Does the categorical view somehow format (positively? negatively?) our understanding? Does the pragmatic approach of computation solve the identity crisis of Logic? It was once thought that classical logic didn’t have any computational interpretation. Then came a quite surprising interpretation given by the logical interpretation of innocent instructions (such as call/cc from Scheme). Can everyday programming provide a significant creativity to Logic? What is the logical interpretation of usual entities of practical programming? Can the practical use of computer science and programming (which is pragmatic) change our understanding of Logic (always influenced by paths of directed, engaged ideas and our understanding of concepts)? It may be interesting to investigate the computational meaning of some mathematical axioms/theorems. How are Logic and Computation related to Cognition and Nature? Linear Logic is used in type systems for quantum functional programming languages but also to describe higher-order quantum circuits through proof nets. How is it connected to quantum computation and quantum information led by the laws of quantum mechanics? Is there really any connection at all? Is it an exaggeration to relate cognition, computation, nature, quantum, and logic? Logic and computation seem to be part of the nature but the nature itself doesn’t seem to truly compute or reason in a discrete framework (or maybe even not at all). More straightforward, are logic and computation part of the nature (i.e does the nature use it as well) or are they a purely artificial exploitation of its potential (e.g logic used as a purely subjective theory of normativity, computation as artificial automation). What is the origin of historical prejudices and misconceptions of Logic? What does the history of Logic teach us about the way humans think and what/why do they believe? Like Physics and Biology, should Logic be taken as a science (observing interaction as a natural phenomenon)? It may be interesting to investigate the biological, psychological and sociological aspects of Logic. Why was Logic thought and organized the way it was? How can the understanding of our failure prevent us from going in wrong directions? What makes something computationally difficult or easy? Implicit Computational Complexity allows to express computational complexity classes by syntactical restrictions on formal languages. It is then interesting to explore the deep structure of these restrictions. It seems that algebraic tools may give an understanding we didn’t have with the algorithmic view. What Logic and Computation really are and how are they similar or different? Even today it is not clear what computation and logic are even though we thought we understood several times. The Curry-Howard isomorphism tells us that logic and (converging coherent?) computation share a common structure and are deeply related. Something similar to what separate analytic and synthetic seem to deeply separate computation and what we call logic. Logic has both a restrictive (type systems à la Curry) and normative (type systems à la Church) effect on computational behaviors (untyped computation) which can be both formulated within Proof Theory thanks to the cut-elimination procedure. What about the purely locative and interactional Turing Machines? Where do they stand? What is the common ground of syntactical formats? Some formalisms took a particular format due to (mainly) historical reasons but are equivalent to other ones. For instance, inductive description of regular languages, finite state automata, regular grammars, regular expressions and read-only Turing machines all define regular languages. How can they be abstracted to only keep interactional potential and use of information/data? Although similar, these formats are far from being strictly/really equivalent. How do they differ? How can we produce a more fertile and simple syntax to describe a concept? How do concepts (specification) become syntax (usable/sharable entities)? Is a study of the administrative aspects of mathematical practice and formats possible? The BHK interpretation is such a concept involving several syntaxes. What is non-determinism and what is its role in the human/machine duality? A kind of non-determinism seems to exists within classical logic especially in the classical Sequent Calculus. Although there are a kind of connection between the two branches of the law of the excluded-middle (prove ~A then focus on A by retraction), to prove a statement of the shape (A or ~A) amount to try to prove A, then ~A, then return to A… (e.g P?=NP). We can’t predict the outcome (if there’s is one). We can also remark that classical logic (in its pure form) lack of computational content (non-confluency) but is the more natural way of reasoning for humans (even though it was rejected by intuitionism). Does all of that have any connection at all with the separation between humans and machines? How does mathematical concepts emerge from thoughts? How does intuitive concepts such as sets, numbers and functions emerges? Numbers for instance, have several definitions (Church, Parigot, Peano, Von Neumann or even composition of morphisms in monoids as unitary categories…). Is there any dialogue within our own mind (subject/subject) or with our environment (subject/object) ensuring this emergence? Is there any (well justified) classes of possible conceptions derived by interactions (either subject/subject or subject/object)? For instance, plurality, unity, existence, universality… What is the correlation between that and our conception of space and time? (are numbers emerging from our conception of time and sets from our conception of space?) What is the point of convergence of the dialectical evolution between humans and machines? The point zero is the point where humans first thought about automatizing their activities (before computers). Let’s call these automatizing mechanisms “machines”. Humans made machines to improve their life and freeing them from repetitive behaviors. The human life which could be defined over a single activity thus evolved by changing its activities. The more machines are improved, the more the concept of human intelligence is redefined. During the era of Artificial Intelligence, machines could approximate human intelligence but then redefined the meaning of intelligence itself in the process. The creation of challenges for machines implicitly lead to the improvement of humanity (always a step ahead of machines). Will the role of human and machines merge more and more through the time? Where does that dialectical evolution lead? Does it create conflicts with social and psychological aspects of humanity? What is the ontological status of the Internet? What is the meaning of the Internet? What does it compute? Thanks to the massive storage of data, the Internet seems to include a record of humanity constantly reflecting its current state. More than that, the Internet seems to globally approximate humanity and its knowledge. Can we see it as an embodiment of a brain of the human kind?
